title: The Agent That Could Not Act
summary: An AI agent can reason through every step of a plan, but when it tries to act, nothing happens. The Lambda function never fires.

resolution.root_cause: The Lambda function's resource-based policy specifies the Bedrock agent's IAM execution role ARN as the aws:SourceArn condition instead of the agent ARN itself, causing Lambda to silently reject invocations from the Bedrock Agents service.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the Lambda resource-based policy has the wrong SourceArn -- it uses the IAM role ARN instead of the Bedrock agent ARN
Player understands that Bedrock Agents falls back to generating responses without the action group when Lambda invocation fails, rather than returning an error
Player proposes updating the Lambda resource-based policy to use the correct agent ARN as the SourceArn condition
Player notes the absence of CloudWatch alarms on Lambda invocation count or Bedrock agent action group failures

learning_objectives: Difference between IAM execution role ARNs and Bedrock agent ARNs in resource-based policies
How Lambda resource-based policies control which services can invoke a function
Bedrock Agents silent fallback behavior when action groups fail
Importance of monitoring action group invocation metrics to detect silent failures
sop_steps: Verify the agent is responding but not using real data. Invoke the Bedrock Agent through the console or CLI and review the trace output. The trace is a step-by-step log of what the agent did. Look for actionGroupInvocationOutput -- if it is missing, the agent never called the Lambda function and made up its answer instead.
Check whether the Lambda function is being called at all. Look at the Invocations metric in CloudWatch for the function. If the count is zero, something is preventing Bedrock from reaching it.

Look at the Lambda function's resource-based policy. A resource-based policy is a permission document attached directly to the function that controls which services are allowed to call it. Use aws lambda get-policy to retrieve it, and inspect the aws:SourceArn condition value -- this is the identity the policy expects the caller to present.

Get the actual Bedrock agent ARN. Use aws bedrock get-agent to find the agent's Amazon Resource Name (ARN), which looks like arn:aws:bedrock:REGION:ACCOUNT:agent/AGENT-ID. This is the unique identifier for the agent as a Bedrock resource.

Compare the two ARNs. The SourceArn in the Lambda policy should match the agent ARN from the previous step. If the policy contains a different ARN (like the IAM execution role ARN -- the role the agent uses internally), Lambda will silently reject every call from Bedrock because the identity does not match.

Fix the policy. Remove the incorrect permission statement using aws lambda remove-permission, then add a new one with the correct agent ARN using aws lambda add-permission. The Principal should be bedrock.amazonaws.com and the SourceArn should be the agent ARN.
Test the fix. Invoke the Bedrock Agent again and check the trace output. Confirm it now shows actionGroupInvocationOutput with real data from the Lambda function, not fabricated answers.

Set up an alarm for zero invocations. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the Lambda function's Invocations metric that triggers when the count drops to zero for one hour during business hours. This catches future silent failures early.

Set up an alarm on the Bedrock side too. Monitor the agent's action group invocation metrics to detect when the agent stops calling its tools. Monitoring both sides -- the caller and the function -- ensures you catch problems regardless of where they originate.

Add an automated integration test. After any policy change, the CI pipeline should invoke the agent and verify the trace contains actionGroupInvocationOutput. A passing policy deployment does not mean the agent can actually reach the function.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The Lambda function has no permission entry for Bedrock at all
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of having the wrong identity in the SourceArn field, the function's resource-based policy simply has no entry for bedrock.amazonaws.com. The effect is the same -- Bedrock cannot call the function, the agent silently falls back to making up answers -- but the fix is adding a new policy statement instead of correcting an existing one.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Include the Lambda resource-based policy in the same infrastructure-as-code template that creates the Bedrock Agent. Validate in CI that the policy exists before marking the deployment as complete.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The action group is turned off or its API definition has errors
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The Lambda function has correct permissions and would work if called. The problem is that the action group (the set of tools the agent can use) is either in a DISABLED state or its API definition (an OpenAPI schema that describes what the tool does) has validation errors. The agent does not know what tool to call, so it skips the action group entirely. CloudWatch shows zero Lambda invocations -- the same symptom as this sim.
related_failure_modes.prevention: After every agent update, verify the action group state is ENABLED. Include API schema validation in the CI pipeline using an OpenAPI linter.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The Lambda function is called but crashes during execution
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Bedrock successfully reaches the function (the resource-based policy is correct), but the function itself fails -- maybe it times out, hits an unhandled error, or the downstream API it calls is down. The Lambda Invocations count is non-zero (so the function is being called), but the Errors count is high. The agent may still fall back to fabricated answers, but at least CloudWatch error metrics give you a signal to investigate.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set the Lambda timeout high enough for the downstream APIs it calls. Add structured error handling in the function code. Create CloudWatch alarms on both Invocations (alert when zero) and Errors (alert when elevated).

related_failure_modes.scenario: The production alias points to an old version of the agent
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The agent's draft version (the editable copy) has the correct action group, but the production alias (the stable pointer that real traffic uses) still points to an older version. That older version may not have the action group or may reference a different Lambda function. The resource-based policy is correct, but traffic is going to the wrong version of the agent.
related_failure_modes.prevention: After preparing a new agent version, always update the alias routing to point to it. Add alias version verification to your deployment checklist.

sop_practices: When giving a Bedrock Agent permission to call a Lambda function, always put the agent ARN (arn:aws:bedrock:REGION:ACCOUNT:agent/AGENT-ID) in the SourceArn field. Do not use the IAM execution role ARN -- that identifies the role the agent uses internally, not the agent itself. Lambda checks the agent ARN when deciding whether to accept the call.

Treat Lambda resource-based policy changes as high-risk. A passing CI pipeline does not mean the function is reachable. After any policy update, verify end-to-end by invoking the agent and confirming the trace shows real data from the function.

Monitor both sides of every service connection. On the Lambda side, watch the Invocations metric to know whether the function is being called. On the Bedrock side, watch action group invocation metrics to know whether the agent is successfully using its tools. Either side can break independently.

Add the aws:SourceAccount condition alongside SourceArn in Lambda resource-based policies. SourceAccount restricts which AWS account the caller must belong to, preventing a confused deputy scenario -- where a malicious actor in a different account tricks the service into calling your function.

glossary.Bedrock Agent: An Amazon Bedrock resource that uses a foundation model to have conversations and take actions on behalf of users. It follows instructions you define and can call external tools through action groups.
glossary.action group: A set of actions a Bedrock Agent can perform. Each action group is backed by a Lambda function or API endpoint that the agent calls when it needs external data or capabilities.
glossary.resource-based policy: A JSON policy document attached directly to an AWS resource (like a Lambda function) that controls which other services or accounts are allowed to interact with it. Separate from IAM role policies.
glossary.aws:SourceArn: A condition key used in resource-based policies to restrict access based on the ARN of the resource that is making the request. Must match the actual caller's ARN exactly.
glossary.foundation model: A large pre-trained AI model (like Claude) hosted on Bedrock. The model generates text responses but has no access to your live data unless it calls an action group.
glossary.Lambda invocation: The act of calling (triggering) a Lambda function to run its code. A service like Bedrock must have permission granted through the function's resource-based policy before Lambda will accept the call.
glossary.silent fallback: A Bedrock Agent behavior where, if an action group call fails, the agent generates an answer from the foundation model alone instead of returning an error. The user sees a confident response with no indication that real data was never fetched.
glossary.IAM execution role: The IAM role that a Bedrock Agent assumes to interact with AWS services on its behalf. Its ARN is different from the agent ARN -- they identify different things.
glossary.agent ARN: The Amazon Resource Name that uniquely identifies a Bedrock Agent as a resource, in the format arn:aws:bedrock:REGION:ACCOUNT:agent/AGENT-ID. This is what Lambda expects in the SourceArn condition, not the IAM role ARN.
glossary.CloudWatch metric: A time-series data point published to Amazon CloudWatch. Metrics like Lambda Invocations or Bedrock InvokeAgent let you monitor whether services are actually being called.

components.name: API Gateway (REST API)
components.role: Receives user queries from the SaaS frontend and routes them to the Bedrock Agent
components.failure_impact: If the API Gateway fails, users cannot reach the AI assistant

components.name: Bedrock Agent (clairvue-bi-assistant, ABCDE12345)
components.role: Orchestrates conversations using Claude 3 Sonnet and the QueryDashboard action group to answer business intelligence questions
components.failure_impact: If the action group invocation fails silently, the agent falls back to hallucinated responses with no error signal

components.name: Lambda function (clairvue-dashboard-query, Python 3.12, arm64)
components.role: Handles action group requests by querying the internal dashboard API and returning real metrics
components.failure_impact: If Lambda rejects the invocation due to a policy mismatch, the agent generates fabricated metrics instead of real data

components.name: IAM execution role (AmazonBedrockExecutionRoleForAgents_clairvue)
components.role: Grants the Bedrock Agent permission to invoke the foundation model and call Lambda, but its ARN is not the SourceArn Lambda expects
components.failure_impact: If confused with the agent ARN in the Lambda policy, invocations are silently rejected

components.name: Lambda resource-based policy
components.role: Controls which services and source ARNs are allowed to invoke the Lambda function
components.failure_impact: If the SourceArn condition references the IAM role ARN instead of the agent ARN, all Bedrock invocations are rejected

components.name: CloudWatch
components.role: Collects Lambda invocation metrics and Bedrock agent invocation metrics for monitoring
components.failure_impact: Without alarms on zero-invocation conditions, silent failures go undetected for days

system.data_flow: User query -> API Gateway -> Bedrock Agent -> (orchestration) -> action group invocation -> Lambda function -> internal dashboard API -> real metrics returned to agent -> agent formats response for user
system.what_broke: The Lambda resource-based policy's SourceArn condition was set to the IAM execution role ARN (arn:aws:iam::847291034651:role/AmazonBedrockExecutionRoleForAgents_clairvue) instead of the agent ARN (arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1:847291034651:agent/ABCDE12345). Lambda rejected every invocation from Bedrock silently. The agent fell back to generating answers from the foundation model alone, producing confident hallucinated metrics for 63 hours.

progressive_clues: agent responds to every query but answers are wrong, Lambda has zero invocations since Friday -- what would cause Bedrock to skip the Lambda call entirely?
Lambda functions require a resource-based policy to allow external services to invoke them -- check who is allowed to call this function and under what conditions
compare the aws:SourceArn value in the Lambda resource-based policy to the actual Bedrock agent ARN in the agent configuration -- are they the same resource?

policy references an IAM role ARN, but Bedrock presents the agent ARN as the source when invoking Lambda -- these are different identifiers, function rejects invocation silently, agent falls back to hallucinating
